quickStart.title: Get productive in 30 seconds
steps: Install the Roo Code VS Code extension and open your project.
Pick a mode: /code for implementation, /architect for planning, /ask for read-only Q&A, /debug for troubleshooting, /orchestrator for delegating subtasks.
Choose a model/provider in Roo settings; Roo remembers the last model per mode with Sticky Models.
Start safe: keep auto-approve off, ask Roo to inspect the codebase, then selectively enable read/edit approvals when you trust the workflow.
Add project MCP servers in .roo/mcp.json when you need external tools, APIs, or internal systems inside Roo.

dailyWorkflows.title: Plan before touching code
dailyWorkflows.description: Switch to Architect mode to gather context and produce an implementation plan without broad write access.
dailyWorkflows.command: /architect Outline the changes needed for this feature, the files involved, and the test plan.

dailyWorkflows.title: Implement with the right model per mode
dailyWorkflows.description: Use Code mode for edits and let Sticky Models swap to your preferred coding model automatically.
dailyWorkflows.command: /code Implement the agreed plan and keep changes scoped to the current task.

dailyWorkflows.title: Debug with a systematic loop
dailyWorkflows.description: Use Debug mode for hypothesis-driven diagnosis, logging, and targeted fixes.
dailyWorkflows.command: /debug Investigate this failing test, identify root cause, then propose the smallest safe fix.

dailyWorkflows.title: Pull in external tools through MCP
dailyWorkflows.description: Connect repo-specific or global MCP servers so Roo can query APIs, docs, databases, or internal tools.
dailyWorkflows.command: Open Roo MCP settings or edit .roo/mcp.json, then ask Roo to use the configured MCP server in-chat.

powerMoves.title: Use Orchestrator for multi-step jobs
powerMoves.description: Break a larger task into subtasks that Roo delegates to specialized modes instead of doing everything in one long thread.
powerMoves.command: /orchestrator Break this feature into planning, implementation, testing, and docs subtasks.
powerMoves.why: You get cleaner context, better mode specialization, and fewer meandering tool calls.

powerMoves.title: Create task-specific custom modes
powerMoves.description: Define a custom mode with restricted tool groups and tailored instructions for recurring work like review, docs, or migrations.
powerMoves.command: Create or edit a mode in Settings > Modes, or ask Roo to create a mode that can only read files and edit Markdown.
powerMoves.why: Custom modes encode team standards and reduce accidental overreach.

powerMoves.title: Pair auto-approve with command allowlists
powerMoves.description: Enable auto-approve only for the permissions and command prefixes you really trust.
powerMoves.command: Toggle auto-approve, select only needed permissions, and restrict execute approval to safe prefixes like git, npm run, or echo.
powerMoves.why: You keep Roo fast during repetitive work without handing it an unrestricted shell.

gotchas.problem: Auto-approve can bypass the confirmation step for file edits, commands, browser actions, and MCP usage.
gotchas.solution: Start with read-only or tightly scoped permissions, keep outside-workspace access off, and use command allow/deny prefixes instead of broad approval.

gotchas.problem: MCP tool auto-approval is two-layered and can be confusing.
gotchas.solution: Enable the global MCP auto-approve setting and the per-tool Always allow checkbox; both must be on for tool auto-approval. Resource access follows the global setting.

gotchas.problem: Project and global MCP configs can conflict.
gotchas.solution: Remember that .roo/mcp.json overrides global mcp_settings.json when server names collide.
Use Ask mode for explanation, Architect for planning, and Code/Debug for changes; if a task seems blocked, confirm the active mode and tool groups first.

gotchas.problem: Command auto-approval can become dangerous if you allow wildcard prefixes.
gotchas.solution: Prefer deny-all plus explicit safe prefixes; keep rm, sudo, git push, publish commands, and destructive shell pipelines denied.

slashCommands.description: Switch to Code mode for normal implementation work with full coding tools.
slashCommands.example: /code Implement the API client and update the tests.
slashCommands.tip: Good default when you already know what to build.

slashCommands.description: Switch to Architect mode for plans, designs, and markdown-first planning.
slashCommands.example: /architect Compare two approaches for adding multitenancy.
slashCommands.tip: Use this before a risky refactor or migration.

slashCommands.description: Switch to Ask mode for read-only explanation and exploration.
slashCommands.example: /ask Explain how authentication currently flows through this app.
slashCommands.tip: Best when you want answers without file mutations.

slashCommands.description: Switch to Debug mode for structured troubleshooting and diagnostics.
slashCommands.example: /debug Why does this test fail only in CI?
slashCommands.tip: Feed it stack traces, failing tests, and reproduction steps.

slashCommands.description: Switch to Orchestrator mode to split complex work into delegated subtasks.
slashCommands.example: /orchestrator Coordinate a release checklist for this repo.
slashCommands.tip: Great for broad tasks that cross planning, coding, and docs.

slashCommands.description: Analyze the codebase and generate Roo assistant configuration and rules files.
slashCommands.tip: Run this early in a repo to give Roo high-signal project context.

config.description: Roo remembers the last-used model for each mode and restores it automatically when you switch back.
config.recommended: Assign stronger reasoning models to Architect/Debug and cheaper faster models to Code/Ask.

config.description: Project-level MCP server configuration shared with the repo.
config.recommended: Keep project-specific servers here so teammates get the same integrations.

config.description: Global MCP server configuration across workspaces.
config.recommended: Use for personal servers; avoid name collisions with project servers unless you want project override behavior.

config.description: Per-capability controls for read, write, execute, browser, MCP, mode switching, subtasks, and follow-up answers.
config.default: Off or limited by user choice
config.recommended: Enable only the minimum set needed for the current task.

config.description: Allowlist/denylist prefixes for command execution when auto-approve for commands is enabled.
config.default: Manual approval when not explicitly allowed
config.recommended: Keep the list narrow; use safe prefixes and explicit denies for destructive commands.

config.description: Project or global custom mode definitions in YAML.
config.recommended: Create focused modes for reviews, docs, migrations, or compliance-heavy tasks.

proTips: Treat Ask and Architect as your safe reconnaissance modes, then switch to Code only after the plan is crisp.
Use project-level MCP config for team-shared tools and global config for personal utilities.
Custom slash commands in .roo/commands/ are great for repeatable review, deploy, and migration prompts.
If Roo feels too eager, check mode permissions before blaming the model.
